entry.message: Community agent run in suggestion mode

window.operatingHours: Agent scans the current UTC day. Live-support hours come from project guidelines or project memory.

items.message:
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

:police_car_light: WAR ROOMS THREAD 07/17/2026 :police_car_light:

These are the available war rooms sessions. Please note that QMs may not always be present; however, the LC community is encouraged to support each other.

We also recommend checking the different breakout rooms during some of the calls, as this may help you connect with other contributors/QMs.

If you encounter any issues with a Zoom session, please tag @manuel.grajales, @lenny.brown, @RADamaris or @jesus.israel in the comments so we can address it promptly.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

items.message:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hi Team!

These CBs have **hil_sandbox** assigned! To proceed, you will need to join the **War Room** so you can coordinate directly with the project team and complete your onboarding.

At this time, there is no scheduled onboarding session. Instead, you may join the War Room whenever it is convenient for you and reach out to any of the available **QMs** in the Zoom. They will be able to assist you with the onboarding process!

**List in thread**

> War room - **hil_sandbox**
> Link: [Meeting Registration -Zoom](https://outlier-ai.zoom.us/j/95330459410?pwd=WnkJA5JTmwg7LcoA2HiiSXUfeuu3P2.1)
> QMs: Yamil Moreno, Carlos Vasquez

Gracias / Obrigado!
Abrazo / Abraço :people_hugging:
items.ignoredReason: Already has a probable reply in the chat or thread.

items.message:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Team!

As many of you have probably noticed, several projects have been wrapping up over the past few days, and some of you may be seeing more empty-queue (EQ) time than usual.

We wanted to reach out ahead of time so you're not caught off guard: **a few of you might be temporarily without an active project** in the coming days. Please don't worry — this is just a short transition. We already have new projects lined up to start over the next days/weeks, and there's a lot of work on the way.

**No need to open a ticket if you're in EQ right now.** We already know who's currently without a project, and we're actively working on the best way to reassign everyone. We'll keep you posted right here as soon as the new projects go live. 🙌

All we ask for is a little patience while things ramp back up. 🙏

Thank you, as always, for the effort you consistently put in. The team is stronger than ever, and we're excited to keep growing together. 💪

Gracias / Obrigado!
Abrazo / Abraço :people_hugging:

probableReplies.message: Thank you very much for letting us know, and especially for all the support! Cheers to all the support team! :saluting_face:

items.ignoredReason: Already has a probable reply in the chat or thread.

decisions.reason: This is a reference document (information sheet) sharing War Room links and project details for multiple projects. It is not a question, request, or message requiring a community management response. The previous message from pilots-coder623 thanking the support team was constructively handled with a reaction.
guidelineSnippets:
As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOC

Instead of writing the file manually, you can use the Rubric Parser Tool. If you want a
detailed step-by-step on how to use it you can read the guide on how to generate the
rubric.json file with the tool, or you can see a full tutorial in the following video.
Step 8 Evaluation Script (return to TOC
Before submitting, you must run your task through the evaluation suite to catch issues the
validation script cannot detect. The suite checks the quality and alignment of your
prompt, tests, rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Download the evaluation suite here: Stargazer_Eval.zip
How to run it (return to TOC
● Unzip Stargazer_Eval.zip into your task's root directory.
Run the Stargazer Eval
● Open the Guide/ folder and follow the HOW_TO_RUN_EVAL.md guidelines.
● Review the consolidated report. The summary table shows a PASS/FAIL verdict for
each component: Prompt, Tests, Rubrics, Alignment, Granularity, Dockerfile, and
Golden Patch.
● If any component returns FAIL, use the “How to Fixˮ table in the report to address
every failed check, then re-run the master eval.
○ Note: If you modify any of the previously uploaded files (e.g.,
test_patch.patch), make sure you also re-run the validation script and
upload the new files
● Only submit your task once the overall verdict is PASS and all components are
review-ready.
This step is mandatory! A task that has not been evaluated with the Stargazer suite is
considered incomplete and may be rejected during review.

Stargazer Axiom Guidelines
Welcome! This document outlines the process and structure for creating issues for the
Stargazer Axiom project, including code base preparation, issue creation, solution
development, test writing, and setting up Docker files and execution scripts.
Table of Contents TOC
Project Overview 2
Task Overview 3

● Should challenge models like Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen 3.5 27b.
● Simple and clear, then add complexity if the model succeeds
Prompt and Rubric Alignment:
At the end of the task, you will write a rubric that can be used to grade at a granular level
how well the model did in solving your issue. The prompt and the rubric need to be
aligned on their level of specificity. This means:
● Prompts must explicitly mention filenames, paths, functions, or modules if the rubric
criteria reference them. If the rubric lists specific paths or APIs, the prompt must also
contain those exact references.
● It is acceptable to make the prompt unrealistically specific if that is necessary to
align with the level of granularity in the rubric and guarantee that the rubric criteria
unambiguously evaluate the solution
How to build a good prompt:
The prompt is the foundation of the task. Its quality determines the quality of every
component built on top of it (test patch, golden patch, and rubrics). Treat this step
carefully.
The goal of the prompt is to realistically simulate an issue a developer would encounter
while modifying or improving a repository. Write from that perspective: a real request,
grounded in the repo, phrased the way a developer would actually phrase it.
Follow these principles:
● Write it like a real GitHub issue. The prompt should read like something a
developer would actually open in a repo focused on one problem or request. Try
not to stack multiple unrelated changes, pile on extra features, or ask for several
different things in the same prompt. One issue, one ask. If you find yourself writing
"and also," stop and split the idea or cut it.
● Be clear and concise. State the problem directly. Difficulty should come from the
realism of the problem, not from artificial complexity or volume.
● Eliminate major ambiguity. Before finalizing, reread the prompt and ask whether
two competent developers would interpret the requirements the same way. If not,
tighten the wording.
● Use explicit requirements. Every requirement must be concrete enough that it can
be verified by a test and satisfied by the gold path solution. Avoid vague language
("make it better," "improve performance") unless the success criteria are clearly
implied by context.
8

items.message:
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

:police_car_light: WAR ROOMS THREAD 07/17/2026 :police_car_light:

These are the available war rooms sessions. Please note that QMs may not always be present; however, the LC community is encouraged to support each other.

We also recommend checking the different breakout rooms during some of the calls, as this may help you connect with other contributors/QMs.

If you encounter any issues with a Zoom session, please tag @manuel.grajales, @lenny.brown, @RADamaris or @jesus.israel in the comments so we can address it promptly.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

items.message:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hi Team!

These CBs have **hil_sandbox** assigned! To proceed, you will need to join the **War Room** so you can coordinate directly with the project team and complete your onboarding.

At this time, there is no scheduled onboarding session. Instead, you may join the War Room whenever it is convenient for you and reach out to any of the available **QMs** in the Zoom. They will be able to assist you with the onboarding process!

**List in thread**

> War room - **hil_sandbox**
> Link: [Meeting Registration -Zoom](https://outlier-ai.zoom.us/j/95330459410?pwd=WnkJA5JTmwg7LcoA2HiiSXUfeuu3P2.1)
> QMs: Yamil Moreno, Carlos Vasquez

Gracias / Obrigado!
Abrazo / Abraço :people_hugging:
items.ignoredReason: Already has a probable reply in the chat or thread.

items.message:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Team!

As many of you have probably noticed, several projects have been wrapping up over the past few days, and some of you may be seeing more empty-queue (EQ) time than usual.

We wanted to reach out ahead of time so you're not caught off guard: **a few of you might be temporarily without an active project** in the coming days. Please don't worry — this is just a short transition. We already have new projects lined up to start over the next days/weeks, and there's a lot of work on the way.

**No need to open a ticket if you're in EQ right now.** We already know who's currently without a project, and we're actively working on the best way to reassign everyone. We'll keep you posted right here as soon as the new projects go live. 🙌

All we ask for is a little patience while things ramp back up. 🙏

Thank you, as always, for the effort you consistently put in. The team is stronger than ever, and we're excited to keep growing together. 💪

Gracias / Obrigado!
Abrazo / Abraço :people_hugging:

probableReplies.message: Thank you very much for letting us know, and especially for all the support! Cheers to all the support team! :saluting_face:

decisions.reason: This is a reference document (information sheet) sharing War Room links and project details for multiple projects. It is not a question, request, or message requiring a community management response. The previous message from pilots-coder623 thanking the support team was constructively handled with a reaction.
guidelineSnippets:
As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOC

Instead of writing the file manually, you can use the Rubric Parser Tool. If you want a
detailed step-by-step on how to use it you can read the guide on how to generate the
rubric.json file with the tool, or you can see a full tutorial in the following video.
Step 8 Evaluation Script (return to TOC
Before submitting, you must run your task through the evaluation suite to catch issues the
validation script cannot detect. The suite checks the quality and alignment of your
prompt, tests, rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Download the evaluation suite here: Stargazer_Eval.zip
How to run it (return to TOC
● Unzip Stargazer_Eval.zip into your task's root directory.
Run the Stargazer Eval
● Open the Guide/ folder and follow the HOW_TO_RUN_EVAL.md guidelines.
● Review the consolidated report. The summary table shows a PASS/FAIL verdict for
each component: Prompt, Tests, Rubrics, Alignment, Granularity, Dockerfile, and
Golden Patch.
● If any component returns FAIL, use the “How to Fixˮ table in the report to address
every failed check, then re-run the master eval.
○ Note: If you modify any of the previously uploaded files (e.g.,
test_patch.patch), make sure you also re-run the validation script and
upload the new files
● Only submit your task once the overall verdict is PASS and all components are
review-ready.
This step is mandatory! A task that has not been evaluated with the Stargazer suite is
considered incomplete and may be rejected during review.

Stargazer Axiom Guidelines
Welcome! This document outlines the process and structure for creating issues for the
Stargazer Axiom project, including code base preparation, issue creation, solution
development, test writing, and setting up Docker files and execution scripts.
Table of Contents TOC

● Should challenge models like Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen 3.5 27b.
● Simple and clear, then add complexity if the model succeeds
Prompt and Rubric Alignment:
At the end of the task, you will write a rubric that can be used to grade at a granular level
how well the model did in solving your issue. The prompt and the rubric need to be
aligned on their level of specificity. This means:
● Prompts must explicitly mention filenames, paths, functions, or modules if the rubric
criteria reference them. If the rubric lists specific paths or APIs, the prompt must also
contain those exact references.
● It is acceptable to make the prompt unrealistically specific if that is necessary to
align with the level of granularity in the rubric and guarantee that the rubric criteria
unambiguously evaluate the solution
How to build a good prompt:
The prompt is the foundation of the task. Its quality determines the quality of every
component built on top of it (test patch, golden patch, and rubrics). Treat this step
carefully.
The goal of the prompt is to realistically simulate an issue a developer would encounter
while modifying or improving a repository. Write from that perspective: a real request,
grounded in the repo, phrased the way a developer would actually phrase it.
Follow these principles:
● Write it like a real GitHub issue. The prompt should read like something a
developer would actually open in a repo focused on one problem or request. Try
not to stack multiple unrelated changes, pile on extra features, or ask for several
different things in the same prompt. One issue, one ask. If you find yourself writing
"and also," stop and split the idea or cut it.
● Be clear and concise. State the problem directly. Difficulty should come from the
realism of the problem, not from artificial complexity or volume.
● Eliminate major ambiguity. Before finalizing, reread the prompt and ask whether
two competent developers would interpret the requirements the same way. If not,
tighten the wording.
● Use explicit requirements. Every requirement must be concrete enough that it can
be verified by a test and satisfied by the gold path solution. Avoid vague language
("make it better," "improve performance") unless the success criteria are clearly
implied by context.
